title: Yogi Ram Suratkumar
subtitle: 1 Dec 1918 – 20 Feb 2001
meta_description: Yogi Ramsuratkumar (1918–2001), affectionately known as 'Bhagawan' and 'Visiri Samiyar' (Saint with hand fan), was an Indian mystic who spent most of his life in Tiruvannamalai, acknowledging Sri Aurobindo, Ramana Maharshi, and Swami Ramdas as his three spiritual fathers.
body_html:
Yogi Ramsuratkumar (1 December 1918 – 20 February 2001), affectionately referred to as 'Bhagawan', was an Indian saint and mystic. He was also referred to as "Visiri Samiyar" (Saint with hand fan) and spent most of his post-enlightenment period in Tiruvannamalai. He, addressing himself as "this beggar", acknowledges the contribution of three saints in his evolution to enlightenment, saying "this beggar has three fathers": Sri Aurobindo of Pondicherry, Sri Ramana Maharishi, and Swami Ramdas of Kanhangad, Kerala.

Yogi Ramsuratkumar (birth name: Ramsurat Kunwar) was born in the village of Naradara, Lalganj, Ballia, near Kashi on 1 December 1918, to Sri Ramdat Kunwar and Smt Kusum Devi. In his childhood he loved very much to meet yogis and monks. He was befriended by a number of holy men and met Sri Sri 1008 Sri Khapadia Baba, a mystic monk, who advised him to go to the south and be a seeker.

He grew up as a Grihasta but eventually, the tugs of spirituality took over. In search of his guru, he visited and spent time in the ashrams of both Sri Aurobindo and Ramana Maharishi. He later moved to Kerala to the ashram of Swami Ramdas. In his own assessment:

  • Sri Aurobindo gave him Jnana (knowledge)
  • Sri Ramana Maharshi blessed him with Tapas (spiritual meditations)
  • Swami Ramdas gave him the nectar of Bhakti (devotion)

Swami Ramdas initiated him into the holy mantra "Om Sri Ram Jai Ram Jai Jai Ram" by pronouncing it thrice in his ears. Yogi Ramsuratkumar refers to this instance as his "death", since from this moment his ego no longer existed and he had a profound spiritual experience.

He finally reached Tiruvannamalai in 1959. He was a "hidden" saint during this early period, seen near the Temple chariot, at the corners of the Road, under the trees of the Temple. As more people started acknowledging the divine presence in him, Swamiji began living in a small house in Sannadhi Street beneath the Temple. His devotees eventually wished him to have an Ashram, which he gently accepted after much persuasion. The Yogi Ramsuratkumar Ashram was constructed at Agrahara collai with a total area of 3.5 Acres.

Bhagawan Yogi Ramsuratkumar said: "I can only say that, whatever exists is one life. Unity: nothing is separate, nothing isolated. This beggar is related to the Sun, to the Moon, to the Infinite Cosmos. This beggar is not limited to this body. All of us who are present here are not separate, isolated. They are part of me. Myself in all these forms. I am the Total. I am the Whole, Absolute, Indivisible, Eternal. Limitless Life, Infinite Life. We are all one together, united, perfect unity in Father. Father in all. That's all I can say."

He also said: "My Father has given a new Name, fresh Name to the world — 'YOGI RAMSURATKUMAR'. It is not the name of this beggar. It is the name of Father. Call upon this name — 'Yogi Ramsuratkumar' and my Father will come for your help at once."
notes: Subtitle is dates. Short page, one image.
